food_subgroup: Stir-fried instant noodles with processed meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Maggi Goreng Hotdog. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (stir-fried noodles) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Maggi Goreng with hotdog in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Maggi Goreng Hotdog or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Maggi Goreng Hotdog as served at a Singapore hawker stall or mamak eatery, approximately 350 g, comprising stir-fried instant noodles with sliced hotdog sausage, egg, vegetables, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng (Indian-style fried noodles) — nutrient composition per 100 g; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central data for hotdog sausage component
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for Indian-style Mee Goreng (fried instant noodles), which is the closest approved primary source for Maggi Goreng. The hotdog sausage component adds modest additional protein and fat; values are adapted proportionally from HPB Mee Goreng baseline with USDA FoodData Central reference for processed pork/chicken sausage (FDC ID 172949). The composite estimate is consistent with the HPB Mee Goreng profile given the hotdog is a minor addition by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — instant noodles / Asian stir-fried noodle dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Maggi Goreng Hotdog. The GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for instant noodles / Asian stir-fried noodle dishes (GI range 47–58, representative value 53). Instant Maggi noodles are the dominant carbohydrate source; the hotdog and egg components do not materially alter the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (53 × 75.25 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 39.9, rounded to 40.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Maggi Goreng Hotdog due to its very high sodium load (~1820 mg per serving, exceeding 75% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg), primarily from instant noodle seasoning packets, soy sauce, and processed hotdog sausage; requesting reduced sauce or sharing a portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~53) but high glycemic load per serving (~40) due to large noodle portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of Maggi Goreng Hotdog is in the low range (~53), the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~75 g available carbohydrate) produces a high glycemic load (~40), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (150–200 g), pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, saturated fat from hotdog sausage, and refined carbohydrate from instant noodles
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Maggi Goreng Hotdog presents a moderate cardiovascular risk due to its combination of high sodium (~1820 mg per serving), saturated fat from the processed hotdog sausage (~7.4 g per serving), and refined carbohydrate from instant noodles; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional intake and consider substituting the hotdog with a leaner protein such as tofu or egg white.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maggi Goreng Hotdog is a popular Singapore hawker and mamak (Indian-Muslim) stall dish consisting of Maggi brand instant noodles stir-fried in a hot wok with sliced hotdog sausage, egg, onion, tomato, green chilli, soy sauce, and tomato ketchup. The dish is a localised variant of Indian-style Mee Goreng, substituting or supplementing the traditional protein (mutton, tofu, or prawn) with a sliced processed hotdog sausage, which adds a mild smoky-savoury flavour and a slightly chewy texture. The noodles are cooked over high heat to achieve a characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) aroma. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet from the ketchup. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 612 kcal, 23.8 g protein, 25.2 g fat, and 75.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high (~1820 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low range), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~40) due to the large noodle portion, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is typically halal-certified at mamak establishments and is a common supper or lunch option in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Maggi Goreng Hotdog?
How much sodium is in Maggi Goreng?
What is the GI of Maggi Goreng?
What is the difference between Mee Goreng and Maggi Goreng?
Where can I find Maggi Goreng Hotdog in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Maggi Goreng Hotdog specifically. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Foodo Indian Mee Goreng entry (closest approved primary source) with minor adjustment for the hotdog sausage component using USDA FoodData Central reference data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for instant noodles / Asian stir-fried noodle dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Indian Mee Goreng as the closest approved primary source; the hotdog sausage addition is estimated to contribute approximately 0.5–1.0 g additional protein and 0.5–1.0 g additional fat per 100 g relative to plain Mee Goreng. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this food category. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this food category. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference data for comparable stir-fried noodle dishes and are not directly reported in HPB Foodo. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for instant noodles; no direct GI measurement for Maggi Goreng Hotdog was found in the approved GI literature. 6. The dish is classified as halal based on typical mamak preparation standards; individual restaurant halal certification should be verified. 7. Serving size of 350 g is based on typical Singapore hawker portion observation; actual restaurant portions may vary. 8. The cuisine tag 'Drinks & Beverages' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform categorisation artefact and has been disregarded for this record.
